court_name: Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit
raw_text: Smith v. Jones, 123 F.3d 456 (9th Cir. 2020). The court held that the defendant failed to establish a genuine issue of material fact. See also Brown v. Board, 347 U.S. 483 (1954). The motion for summary judgment is GRANTED pursuant to Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 56(c). The district court ruling is AFFIRMED.

court_name: Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit
raw_text: United States v. Williams, 456 F.3d 789 (2d Cir. 2019). The Fourth Amendment prohibits unreasonable searches and seizures. See Mapp v. Ohio, 367 U.S. 643 (1961). The exclusionary rule applies here.
text: United States v. Williams, 456 F.3d 789 (2d Cir. 2019). The Fourth Amendment prohibits unreasonable searches and seizures. See Mapp v. Ohio, 367 U.S. 643 (1961). The exclusionary rule applies here. Evidence obtained in violation of constitutional rights must be suppressed. The trial court correctly applied the exclusionary rule to bar admission of the evidence. We affirm the district court decision in its entirety. court_name: Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit
raw_text: Johnson v. City of Dallas, 789 F.3d 123 (5th Cir. 2021). Section 1983 provides a cause of action for deprivation of constitutional rights under color of state law. Monroe v. Pape, 365 U.S. 167 (1961).
text: Johnson v. City of Dallas, 789 F.3d 123 (5th Cir. 2021). Section 1983 provides a cause of action for deprivation of constitutional rights under color of state law. Monroe v. Pape, 365 U.S. 167 (1961). The qualified immunity doctrine shields government officials from liability unless they violated clearly established statutory or constitutional rights. The plaintiff bears the burden of demonstrating that the right was clearly established at the time of the violation. We reverse the district court and remand for further proceedings. court_name: Court of Appeals for the Fourth Circuit
raw_text: Davis v. Maryland, 321 F.3d 654 (4th Cir. 2018). The Commerce Clause grants Congress broad authority to regulate economic activity that substantially affects interstate commerce. Wickard v. Filburn, 317 U.S. 111 (1942).
text: Davis v. Maryland, 321 F.3d 654 (4th Cir. 2018). The Commerce Clause grants Congress broad authority to regulate economic activity that substantially affects interstate commerce. Wickard v. Filburn, 317 U.S. 111 (1942). The substantial effects test has been applied broadly to uphold federal regulation of local activities that in aggregate substantially affect interstate commerce. The dissent argues the majority opinion extends federal power beyond its constitutional limits. The constitutional boundaries of the Commerce Clause remain contested. court_name: Court of Appeals for the Eleventh Circuit
raw_text: Taylor v. Healthcare Corp, 654 F.3d 987 (11th Cir. 2022). ERISA preempts state law claims that relate to an employee benefit plan. Shaw v. Delta Air Lines, 463 U.S. 85 (1983). Complete preemption applies.
text: Taylor v. Healthcare Corp, 654 F.3d 987 (11th Cir. 2022). ERISA preempts state law claims that relate to an employee benefit plan. Shaw v. Delta Air Lines, 463 U.S. 85 (1983). Complete preemption applies where the cause of action falls within the scope of ERISA. The plaintiff state law claims are completely preempted by ERISA and must be recharacterized as federal claims. The district court correctly dismissed the state law claims as preempted. We affirm the judgment of the district court in all respects. The scope of ERISA preemption is broad and encompasses all state law claims that relate to benefit plans. The plaintiff state law claims are completely preempted by ERISA and must be recharacterized as federal claims. The district court correctly dismissed the state law claims as preempted.
